planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; German Stripe is a large Mennonite heirloom beefsteak tomato with stunning bicolor red and gold-yellow striped and marbled flesh — the visual effect when the fruit is sliced is extraordinary, with crimson and golden streaks radiating through the flesh in an irregular pattern, accompanied by an outstandingly rich, honey-sweet flavor.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant outdoors after frost danger passes into fertile, deeply prepared, well-drained soil; provide substantial staking or caging as plants reach 5–8 feet. German Stripe (also sometimes called 'Old German' or 'Old German Stripe') is a large Mennonite heirloom beefsteak tomato of the bicolor class — producing very large (12–28 oz), flattened to slightly lobed fruits that are visually unremarkable from the outside (reddish-gold exterior) but stunning when sliced: the interior flesh is dramatically streaked and marbled with alternating crimson red and golden yellow in bold, irregular patterns radiating from the blossom scar, making each cross-section unique and visually captivating. The flavor matches the dramatic appearance — exceptionally sweet and rich with a honey-complexity that is one of the hallmarks of the best bicolor heirlooms; the sweetness from the golden areas contrasts with the more acid-forward richness of the red portions to create a layered, complex taste. One of the finest beefsteak heirlooms for fresh eating, tomato salads, and caprese where the bicolor slices can be displayed to advantage.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; Black Prince is a Siberian heirloom dark tomato producing medium-sized, round to slightly oblate fruits with dark red-purple to almost-black coloring at the shoulders that transitions to deep burgundy-red — an early, flavorful representative of the 'black tomato' category with a rich, earthy, complex taste profile associated with the high anthocyanin content of dark-skinned varieties.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; Black Prince performs exceptionally well in cool climates due to its Siberian origin and can be transplanted earlier than most heat-requiring varieties. Black Prince is a Russian heirloom tomato from Siberia, where it was developed to produce ripe fruits under cool growing conditions with a shorter summer season than most tomato-growing regions — a characteristic that makes it one of the earliest-maturing of the 'dark tomato' class, which typically run late. The medium (3–6 oz), round to slightly flattened fruits develop dark red-purple to near-black pigmentation in sun-exposed areas of the skin (from anthocyanin development under UV exposure), while shaded areas remain deep burgundy-red; the interior flesh is dark red to mahogany with dense, meaty texture and a distinctively complex flavor profile — sweet and rich with earthy, slightly smoky undertones and good acid balance. Black Prince is an excellent introduction to the 'dark tomato' flavor category for gardeners unfamiliar with the type; it is earlier, more compact, and more productive than larger dark varieties like Cherokee Purple or Paul Robeson, making it particularly suitable for containers, short seasons, and cooler gardens.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; Siletz is an Oregon State University development bred specifically for cool, short-season growing in the Pacific Northwest, setting fruit at temperatures below 55°F when standard varieties fail, and producing large red beefsteak-style fruits in as few as 52 days from transplant — ideal for cloudy coastal gardens where summers are cool and brief.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; Siletz can be transplanted 1–2 weeks before last frost in mild climates due to its cold tolerance for a tomato; it is determinate and does not require staking, though a cage helps manage the heavy fruit load. Siletz was developed by Dr. Jim Baggett at Oregon State University as part of the Pacific Northwest vegetable breeding program, named for the Siletz River in Oregon's Coast Range; it was released specifically to address the challenge of growing tomatoes in the cool, overcast summers of the Pacific Coast where nighttime temperatures regularly fall below the fruit-set threshold of standard varieties. The large (8–12 oz), round to slightly flattened, bright red fruits have good meaty texture and pleasant, balanced sweet-acid flavor — not the most complex among heirloom varieties, but reliably excellent given its exceptional earliness and cold tolerance. In Gulf Coast gardens, Siletz makes an outstanding early-season variety when planted in February–March, producing ripe fruit well before the onset of summer heat that terminates most tomato production. Its determinate growth habit allows clean harvest before the hot season, with plants ripening a concentrated crop over 3–4 weeks.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; Sioux was developed by the University of Nebraska and released in 1944 as a high-quality, heat-tolerant variety specifically for hot climates of the American Midwest, Great Plains, and South — it continues setting fruit at high temperatures where many other varieties stop production, making it a historically important variety for hot-summer gardens.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost into fertile, well-drained soil; provide staking as plants grow 4–6 feet. Sioux was released in 1944 by the Nebraska Agricultural Experiment Station as a significant improvement over the standard open-pollinated varieties of the era, specifically bred and selected for the ability to continue setting and sizing fruit through the hot, dry summers of the Great Plains and Southern states — a characteristic that remained unusual enough in the mid-20th century to make Sioux a widely recommended variety for hot climates for many decades. The medium (6–8 oz), round to slightly oblate, smooth-skinned, uniformly deep red fruits have classic bright, balanced sweet-acid tomato flavor with good texture for fresh slicing or canning; flavor quality is highest when night temperatures drop below 70°F. Sioux is an indeterminate variety with continued production through summer; in the Gulf Coast region it provides good spring production (February–May planting) and can extend into early summer better than most varieties. One of the few mid-20th century university-bred heirlooms now available through seed savers.
sources: University of Nebraska Agricultural Experiment Station

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; Legend is an Oregon State University tomato released in 1996 with the highest level of late blight (Phytophthora infestans) resistance available in an open-pollinated tomato variety — developed specifically for the damp, foggy conditions of the Pacific Coast where late blight is a persistent annual challenge that can devastate unprotected tomato crops.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; Legend is determinate and does not require staking, though a cage prevents fruit from resting on the soil; provides excellent production without fungicide sprays in conditions where late blight is present. Legend was released in 1996 by Dr. Jim Baggett at Oregon State University, culminating many years of breeding for late blight resistance in open-pollinated tomatoes; at the time of release it had the highest documented level of late blight resistance in any open-pollinated variety, making it a breakthrough for organic and low-spray gardeners in humid, blight-prone climates. The large (10–16 oz), round to slightly flattened, smooth, uniform deep red fruits have thick walls, good meaty texture, and classic sweet-balanced tomato flavor superior to most disease-resistant commercial varieties; the determinate plant ripens a heavy concentrated crop over 3–4 weeks rather than producing continuously. Legend performs exceptionally well in the Pacific Northwest and humid Eastern climates; it is also valuable in Gulf Coast gardens planted in fall (August–September) when late blight risk from summer thunderstorm humidity is highest. One of the most important contributions to open-pollinated tomato breeding in the late 20th century.
